summary:
The State of Israel declared independence on May 14, 1948, as the British Mandate for Palestine expired. David Ben-Gurion, the head of the Jewish Agency, read the Israeli Declaration of Independence in Tel Aviv, proclaiming the establishment of a Jewish state in the Land of Israel. The United States recognized the new state within eleven minutes of the declaration; the Soviet Union followed three days later. The establishment of Israel fulfilled the Zionist dream of a Jewish homeland in historic Palestine but simultaneously launched a conflict with the Arab world that would define Middle Eastern politics for decades.

The roots of the conflict stretched back to the late nineteenth century, when the Zionist movement began organizing Jewish immigration to Ottoman-controlled Palestine. The [[event:2752|Balfour Declaration]] of 1917 pledged British support for a Jewish national home in Palestine while promising to protect the civil and religious rights of the existing non-Jewish population — two promises that proved impossible to reconcile simultaneously. Jewish immigration accelerated dramatically after the Holocaust, and by 1947, Palestine had approximately 600,000 Jews and 1.2 million Palestinian Arabs.

Unable to resolve the conflict, Britain referred the question to the [[event:2663|United Nations]], which in November 1947 proposed a partition plan dividing Palestine into Jewish and Arab states with Jerusalem under international administration. Jewish leaders accepted the plan; Arab states rejected it. As British forces withdrew in May 1948, Jewish forces — organized under the Haganah military organization — had already secured substantial territory through military operations.

The day after independence was declared, the [[event:2673|Arab-Israeli War of 1948]] began, as Egypt, Jordan, Iraq, Syria, and Lebanon entered the conflict. The war ended with Israeli forces in control of approximately 78 percent of the former mandatory territory, more than the UN partition plan had allocated. About 700,000 Palestinian Arabs fled or were expelled in what Palestinians call the Nakba (catastrophe), creating a refugee crisis whose resolution remained contested into the twenty-first century.

Israel's founding had been shaped significantly by the Holocaust: international sympathy for Jewish survivors and revulsion at the genocide of European Jewry created political pressure for a Jewish state. Survivors of the Holocaust formed a large proportion of the early immigrants. The Israeli government made Holocaust remembrance central to national identity, establishing Yad Vashem, the World Holocaust Remembrance Center, in 1953.

The creation of Israel remains one of the most contested events of the twentieth century. For Israelis and Jews worldwide, it represented national rebirth and refuge after millennia of persecution. For Palestinians, it was a catastrophe that dispossessed hundreds of thousands and left unresolved questions of sovereignty, refugees, and statehood that continued to drive conflict. The establishment of a permanent Israeli state and the failure to establish a Palestinian state became the central unresolved political problem of the modern Middle East.
